title: Diffusion Models × Natural Language × Genomic Sequences — Knowledge Graph
thesis: Diffusion models began as a continuous-noise generative process for images but have rapidly extended to discrete tokens (text, DNA, RNA, protein sequences) and to high-dimensional structured biology. We argue that progress at this intersection will be driven by three coordinated developments: (i) continuous-time diffusion foundations (score-based modelling, classifier-free guidance, latent-space scaling) that supply the modelling vocabulary; (ii) discrete & language diffusion that adapts the framework to text and other token sequences and now reaches LLM scale; and (iii) biological sequence and structure diffusion that translates these advances into protein, DNA, and RNA design — closing the loop between natural-language conditioning and functional biomolecule generation.

paradigms.label: Continuous diffusion foundations
paradigms.summary: The modelling vocabulary: score-based generative modelling, discrete-time denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPM), and the continuous-time SDE framework; classifier-free guidance and latent-space scaling that made diffusion practical at modern scale.

claims.claim: Score-based generative modelling and denoising diffusion probabilistic models (NCSN, DDPM) establish a learnable noise-prediction objective whose reverse process samples high-quality data from a tractable Gaussian prior, giving the field its core modelling vocabulary.

useful_outcomes.rationale: Ho et al. DDPM paper directly introduces the noise-prediction epsilon-objective and beats GAN-class samplers on CIFAR-10 and LSUN unconditional synthesis.

immediate_benefit.rationale: The simple epsilon-prediction training loop and reverse-process sampler are still the canonical recipe adopted by virtually every downstream diffusion implementation.

plausible.rationale: Derives the simplified variational bound from first principles and shows tight empirical agreement between predicted noise and high-quality samples.

scalable.rationale: Architecture is a standard UNet trained at image resolution; scaling beyond CIFAR/LSUN was demonstrated only by follow-up work.

how_to_validate.rationale: Reports FID/IS on held-out splits and likelihoods, the protocol that became the de facto evaluation standard for diffusion samplers.

paradigms.label: Discrete & language diffusion
paradigms.summary: Adapting diffusion to discrete tokens — first via D3PM-style discrete state Markov chains, then via score-entropy / masked diffusion that scales much better, and finally via LLM-scale discrete diffusion models that challenge autoregressive language modelling head-on.

claims.claim: Discrete-state diffusion (D3PM, DiffusionLM) defines a forward Markov chain over discrete tokens — uniform / absorbing / Gaussian transition kernels — and trains a reverse denoising model that can generate fluent text without an autoregressive factorisation.

useful_outcomes.rationale: Diffusion-LM is the named exemplar in the claim and shows controllable non-autoregressive text generation outperforming prior plug-and-play baselines on six control tasks.

immediate_benefit.rationale: Released training and control recipe lets practitioners apply continuous-embedding diffusion to controllable NLG immediately.

plausible.rationale: Continuous-embedding relaxation is principled but adds rounding noise versus a pure discrete Markov chain.

scalable.rationale: Trained at small (~80M) scale on E2E/ROC; scaling to LLM regime not demonstrated.

how_to_validate.rationale: Controllable-generation benchmark suite (syntax, semantics) provides a transferable evaluation protocol.

evidence_points.text: Diffusion-LM Improves Controllable Text Generation: Diffusion-LM is the named exemplar in the claim and shows controllable non-autoregressive text generation outperforming prior plug-and-play baselines on six control tasks.

sentences.text: Diffusion-LM allows us to perform these control tasks without a classiﬁer, and our Diffusion-LM signiﬁcantly outperforms prior plug-and-play methods and is on-par with an autoregressive LM trained from scratch for the inﬁlling task (§7.3).
The goal of controllable generation is to generate w that satisﬁes the control target c.

Consider the plug-and-play controllable generation setting: we are given a language model plm(w) trained from a large amount of unlabeled text data, and for each control task, we are given a classiﬁer p(c | w) trained from smaller amount of labeled text data (e.g., for syntactic control, the classiﬁer is a probabilistic parser).
Having described the Diffusion-LM, we now consider the problem of controllable text generation (§5.1) and decoding (§5.2).

immediate_benefit.rationale: Partially-noising classifier-free seq2seq recipe is directly reusable for conditional text generation.

plausible.rationale: Builds on embedding-space diffusion with sound partial-noise objective; relies on continuous relaxation.

paradigms.label: Biological sequence & structure diffusion
paradigms.summary: Translating diffusion into protein, DNA, and RNA design. Three sub-areas have reached production-grade utility: structure-conditioned protein backbone generation (RFdiffusion), protein-sequence diffusion language models (EvoDiff / DPLM), and nucleotide-sequence diffusion for DNA / RNA design (DNADiffusion, DiscDiff, RNAdiffusion, DRAKES).

claims.claim: Diffusion over 3D protein backbones (RFdiffusion-style) enables de novo design of binders, scaffolds, and symmetric assemblies that experimentally fold and function — bringing diffusion-as-generative-prior into the wet-lab design loop.

useful_outcomes.rationale: RFdiffusion experimentally validates hundreds of designed symmetric assemblies, metal-binding proteins, and binders (including a cryo-EM-confirmed influenza HA binder) — direct, wet-lab-verified benefit over prior hallucination/MCMC baselines for this exact claim.

immediate_benefit.rationale: Code, weights, and pipelines (RFdiffusion + ProteinMPNN) are publicly released and adopted across the design community; a researcher can run it today on standard GPUs.

plausible.rationale: Fine-tuning RoseTTAFold for structure denoising is methodologically sound, with extensive in silico ablations and three independent experimental classes (oligomers, binders, metal sites) corroborating the model.

sentences.text: To enable this, we introduce a diffusion process that respects the conformational statistics of polymer ensembles, an efficient neural architecture for molecular systems that enables long-range reasoning with sub-quadratic scaling, layers for efficiently synthesizing three-dimensional structures of proteins from predicted inter-residue geometries and a general low-temperature sampling algorithm for diffusion models.
The experimental characterization of 310 proteins shows that sampling from Chroma results in proteins that are highly expressed, fold and have favourable biophysical properties.

Finally, we introduce methods for low-temperature sampling with a modified diffusion process that allows us to trade an increased quality of sam- pled backbones (increasing likelihood) for reduced conformational diversity (reducing entropy; Supplementary Figs.
5h), indicating that proteins with various structural compositions can be designed by Chroma.

Its efficient design, with an innovative diffusion process, quasilinear scaling neural architecture and low-temperature sam- pling method, means that Chroma can generate extremely large proteins and protein complexes (with more than 3,000 residues) on a commodity graphics processing unit (such as an NVIDIA V100) in a few minutes.

sentences.text: De novo protein design can now generate binders with high affinity and specificity to structured proteins6,7.

We show that by extending RFdiffusion8 to enable binder design to flexible targets, and to refining input structure models by successive noising and denoising (partial diffusion), picomolar-affinity binders can be generated to helical peptide targets by either refining designs generated with other methods, or completely de novo starting from random noise distributions without any subsequent experimental optimization.

RFdiffusion was pre- viously shown to be able to design binders to folded targets8; here we demonstrate further that it can be used to improve starting designs by partial noising and denoising, and can generate binders to peptides starting from no information other than the target sequence.
Approximately 2,000 partially diffused designs were generated for each target.
This version of RFdiffusion was trained for seven epochs.

sentences.text: In this study, we present DynamicBind, a deep learning method that employs equivariant geometric diffusion networks to construct a smooth energy landscape, promoting efﬁcient transitions between different equili- brium states.

DynamicBind can capture ligand-speciﬁc protein conforma- tional changes Conventional docking protocols usually perform protein conforma- tion sampling as a separate step from the docking process15,38.
These results demonstrate that, DynamicBind, is capable of capturing ligand-speciﬁc conformational changes.
These advantages empower DynamicBind for the virtual screening of com- pounds that bind to cryptic pockets.
In addition to translation and rotation, torsion angles are also crucial factors in determining the ligand conformation.

sentences.text: Abstract This paper introduces diffusion protein language model (DPLM), a versatile protein language model that demonstrates strong generative and predictive capabilities for protein sequences.

We first pre-train scalable DPLMs from evolutionary- scale protein sequences within a generative self- supervised discrete diffusion probabilistic frame- work, which generalizes language modeling for proteins in a principled way.
After pre-training, DPLM exhibits the ability to generate struc- turally plausible, novel and diverse protein se- quences for unconditional generation.
DPLM is grounded in a discrete diffusion probabilistic framework, serving as a principled generative generalization of language modeling.
This indicates that DPLM excels in motif-scaffolding, preserv- ing the motif structure during scaffold generation.

sentences.text: Our approach not only sets new standards in DNA sequence generation but also demonstrates supe- rior performance over existing diffusion models, in generating both short and long DNA sequences.
DiscDiff surpasses existing state-of-the-art diffusion models in short and long DNA generation by 7.6% and 1.9%, respectively, as measured by the motif distribution.
We introduce DiscDiff, a novel, and to our knowledge the first Latent Discrete Diffusion model for DNA generation tasks.
In Sec- tion 5.2, we compare DiscDiff with the baseline diffusion models on unconditional generations.
DiscDiff produces the most realistic DNA sequences compared to other diffusion baselines on both short and long-sequence generations.
